instruction: Extract all core computer science concepts from the following lecture JSON. Return only a newline-separated list of unique concepts. Do not explain anything.

text_blocks.text: Discrete Structures for Computer
Science

bill@cs.pitt.edu
6311 Sennott Square
Lecture #7: Proof Techniques
Based on materials developed by Dr. Adam Lee

text_blocks.text: Direct proof
In a direct proof, we prove p →q by showing that if p is

runs.text: q by showing that if p is

text_blocks.text: true, then q must necessarily be true
Example: Prove that if n is an odd integer, then n2is

runs.text: Prove that if n is an odd integer, then n

text_blocks.text: an odd integer.
Proof:
l Assume that n is odd.  That is n = (2k + 1) for some integer k.

runs.text: Assume that n is odd.  That is n = (2k + 1) for some integer k.

text_blocks.text: l Note that n2= (2k+1)2= (4k2+ 4k + 1)
l We can factor the above to get 2(2k2+ 2k) +  1

input.title: Direct proofs are not always the easiest way to

text_blocks.text: Direct proofs are not always the easiest way to

runs.text: Direct proofs are not always the easiest way to

text_blocks.text: prove a given conjecture.
In this case, we can try proof by contraposition

runs.text: In this case, we can try

text_blocks.text: How does this work?
l Recall that p →q ≡¬q →¬p
l Therefore, a proof of ¬q →¬p is also a proof of p →q

runs.text: ¬p is also a proof of p

text_blocks.text: Proof by contraposition is an indirect proof technique
since we don’t prove p →q directly.
Let’s take a look at an example…

runs.text: Let’s take a look at an example…

images.ai_description: The image displays the University of Pittsburgh emblem featuring a shield with castles and checkered pattern, encircled by the university name, "Veritas" and "Virtus."

text_blocks.text: Prove: If n is an integer and 3n + 2 is odd, then

runs.text: If n is an integer and 3n + 2 is odd, then

text_blocks.text: n is odd.
First, attempt a direct proof:
l Assume that 3n + 2 is odd, thus 3n + 2 = 2k + 1 for some k

runs.text: Assume that 3n + 2 is odd, thus 3n + 2 = 2k + 1 for some k

text_blocks.text: l Can solve to find that n = (2k – 1)/3

runs.text: Can solve to find that n = (2k – 1)/3

text_blocks.text: Now, try proof by contraposition:
l Assume n is even, so n = 2k for some k

runs.text: Assume n is even, so n = 2k for some k

text_blocks.text: l So, 3n + 2 is also even.
l Since we proved ¬“n is odd” →¬“3n+2 is odd”, we can
conclude that “3n + 2 is odd” →“n is odd” ☐
Where do we go from here?!?

runs.text: Where do we go from here?!?

images.ai_description: Seal of the University of Pittsburgh featuring a shield with checkered design, castle icons, the words "Veritas" and "Virtus," and the year 1787.

Assume that p ∧¬q is true
Proceed with the proof
If this assumption leads us to a contradiction, we can

runs.text: If this assumption leads us to a contradiction, we can

text_blocks.text: conclude that p →q is true
Let’s revisit an earlier example…

text_blocks.text: When trying to prove a new conjecture, a good “meta

runs.text: When trying to prove a new conjecture, a good “meta

text_blocks.text: strategy” is to:
If possible, try to reuse an existing proof (analogy!)

runs.text: If possible, try to reuse an existing proof (analogy!)

text_blocks.text: If the conjecture looks fishy, check for a counterexample

runs.text: If the conjecture looks fishy, check for a counterexample

text_blocks.text: Attempt a “real” proof
a)
Apply the forward reasoning strategy
b)
Or, apply the backward reasoning strategy

runs.text: Or, apply the backward reasoning strategy

text_blocks.text: c)
Possibly alternate between forward and backward reasoning

runs.text: Possibly alternate between forward and backward reasoning

text_blocks.text: Unfortunately, not every proof can be solved using this

text_blocks.text: nice little meta strategy…
In fact, there are many, many proof strategies out there, and

text_blocks.text: NONE of them can be guaranteed to find a proof!!!
